text:
{
  "title": "Substance Use Buffering Context",
  "group_name": "substance_use_context_buffer",
  "family": "behavioral_risk_context",
  "summary": "Represent whether substance-use risk is buffered or amplified by the surrounding lifestyle context, separating students whose smoking/alcohol pattern co-occurs with protective habits from those where it clusters with broader health-risk behavior.",
  "depends_on": [],
  "strategy": "Map smoking_alcohol to an ordinal substance burden: no=0, occasional=1, yes=2, missing=-1. Build a protective_context_score as the count of satisfied supports: diet_type in {balanced, veg}, physical_activity_level in {moderate, active}, sleep_quality=good, stress_level=low, 7 <= sleep_duration <= 9, step_count >= 8000, exercise_duration >= 30, 18.5 <= bmi < 25. Build an adverse_context_score as the count of satisfied risks: stress_level=high, sleep_quality=poor, physical_activity_level=sedentary, diet_type=non-veg, sleep_duration < 6 or sleep_duration > 9.5, step_count < 5000, exercise_duration < 10, bmi < 18.5 or bmi >= 30, heart_rate >= 90. Missing values do not satisfy protective or adverse conditions, except smoking_alcohol missing receives the explicit missing burden code. Derive substance_buffer_gap = adverse_context_score + substance_burden - protective_context_score when substance_burden >= 0, otherwise null. Derive substance_amplification = substance_burden * adverse_context_score and substance_buffering = substance_burden * protective_context_score, with nulls when substance_burden is missing. Add one categorical context bucket: missing_substance, clean_supported for no use with protective_context_score >= 5 and adverse_context_score <= 1, non_substance_lifestyle_risk for no use with adverse_context_score >= 4, buffered_use for occasional/yes with protective_context_score >= 4 and adverse_context_score <= 2, compounding_use for occasional/yes with adverse_context_score >= 4, and mixed_context otherwise.",
  "expected_signal": "Student health literature and AHA lifestyle guidance support that substance use, activity, diet, sleep, BMI, and stress cluster as joint risk patterns, so this group may sharpen balanced accuracy by distinguishing fit-like protective profiles, at-risk buffered-use profiles, and unhealthy compounding-risk profiles using only observed features.",
  "risk": "This may be partly redundant with broad lifestyle-burden features, and fixed clinical-style cutoffs can be unstable if the competition labels were generated from different thresholds or if smoking_alcohol combines smoking and alcohol in a way that weakens the ordinal assumption."
}
